title: Family
description: Talk about your family, relationships and household

rows.label: Family
rows.english: Family — Slovaks are close-knit. 'Rodinný večer' (family evening) is a valued tradition

rows.label: Mum
rows.english: Mum / Mother (informal). More formal: Matka. 'Mama' is what most Slovaks say day-to-day

rows.label: Dad
rows.english: Father / Dad. Informal: Tato or Tatko. 'Otec' is formal; 'Tato' is what children typically say

rows.label: Parents
rows.english: Parents (plural of rodič). 'Tvoji rodičia' = your parents. Used as a unit: 'Moji rodičia sú z Bratislavy'

rows.label: Brother
rows.english: Brother. Starší brat = older brother. Mladší brat = younger brother

rows.label: Sister
rows.english: Sister. Staršia sestra = older sister. Mladšia sestra = younger sister

rows.label: Husband
rows.english: Husband. 'Môj manžel' = my husband. A married couple are called 'manželia'

rows.label: Wife
rows.english: Wife. 'Moja manželka' = my wife. The female form adds the -ka suffix

rows.label: Boyfriend
rows.english: Boyfriend / male friend (context makes it clear). 'Môj priateľ' = my boyfriend

rows.label: Girlfriend
rows.english: Girlfriend / female friend. 'Moja priateľka' = my girlfriend. The female -ka suffix again!

rows.label: Son
rows.english: Son. 'Môj syn má päť rokov' = My son is five years old

rows.label: Daughter
rows.english: Daughter. 'Moja dcéra' = my daughter. Note: dcéra has a special pronunciation — the 'dc' cluster!

rows.label: Grandpa
rows.english: Grandpa / Grandfather (informal). Formal: Starý otec. Children almost always say 'Dedko'

rows.label: Grandma
rows.english: Grandma / Grandmother. Formal: Stará mama. Babička is universally used and beloved in Slovak culture

rows.label: Uncle
rows.english: Uncle (mother's or father's brother). Also: Ujo (more informal, especially in western Slovakia)

rows.label: Aunt
rows.english: Aunt. 'Teta Mária' = Aunt Mária. Teta can also mean 'lady' informally (to a child's teacher, for example)

rows.label: Cousin (male)
rows.english: Male cousin (literally 'little brother' — from brat). Slovak distinguishes male and female cousins!

rows.label: Cousin (female)
rows.english: Female cousin (from sestra = sister). The gender distinction is important in Slovak!

rows.label: Brother-in-law
rows.english: Brother-in-law (wife's or husband's brother)

rows.label: Sister-in-law
rows.english: Sister-in-law (wife's or husband's sister)

rows.label: Father-in-law
rows.english: Father-in-law. The in-laws collectively are called 'svokrovci'

rows.label: Mother-in-law
rows.english: Mother-in-law. A famously sensitive relationship in Slovak culture — svokra jokes are plentiful!

rows.label: Siblings
rows.english: Siblings — brothers and sisters collectively. 'Nemám súrodencov' = I have no siblings

rows.label: Single (M/F)
rows.english: Single (male: slobodný, female: slobodná). The -ý/-á ending changes based on gender

rows.label: Married (M/F)
rows.english: Married — different words! Ženatý = married man, Vydatá = married woman. Both correct, context-dependent

rows.label: Divorced (M/F)
rows.english: Divorced (male: rozvedený, female: rozvedená)

rows.label: Widower/Widow
rows.english: Widower (male: vdovec) / Widow (female: vdova)

rows.label: Engaged
rows.english: We are engaged. (zasnúbení = engaged, plural form)

rows.label: Expecting
rows.english: We are expecting a baby. (literally 'we are waiting for a child')

rows.label: Only child (M/F)
rows.english: Only child (male: jedináčik, female: jedináčka). 'Som jedináčik' = I'm an only child (male)

rows.label: Big/small family
rows.english: I have a big/small family. (veľkú = big, malú = small — accusative forms)

rows.label: Parents' home
rows.english: Where do your parents live? A common question when getting to know someone in Slovakia

scenarios.situation: A Slovak colleague asks 'Si ženatý?' What are they asking you?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Si ženatý? = Are you married? (asked to a male). A woman would be asked 'Si vydatá?' — ženatý is for men, vydatá is for women. This gender distinction is important in Slovak grammar!

scenarios.situation: You want to say 'I have an older brother and a younger sister.' Which is correct?
choices: Mám mladšieho brata a staršiu sestru.
scenarios.answer: Mám mladšieho brata a staršiu sestru.
scenarios.answerMeaning: Mám staršieho brata a mladšiu sestru = I have an older brother and a younger sister. Starší = older, Mladší = younger. Slovak adjectives change ending to match the noun's gender — brata (masculine), sestru (feminine).

scenarios.situation: How does a male speaker say 'I'm an only child' in Slovak?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Som jedináčik = I'm an only child (male). A female would say 'Som jedináčka.' Jedináčik comes from jediný = only/sole. It's a common topic in family conversations — Slovaks often ask 'Máš súrodencov?' (Do you have siblings?).

scenarios.situation: Your Slovak neighbour asks 'Kde bývajú tvoji rodičia?' What do they want to know?
choices: What do your parents do for work?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Kde bývajú tvoji rodičia? = Where do your parents live? Kde = where, bývajú = they live, tvoji rodičia = your parents. This is one of the first questions Slovaks ask when getting to know you — family and origins matter a lot in Slovak culture.

scenarios.situation: You want to introduce your girlfriend to a Slovak colleague. Which phrase do you use?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Toto je moja priateľka = This is my girlfriend. Priateľka = girlfriend / female friend. Manželka = wife (you're married). Sestra = sister. Context usually clarifies whether priateľka means 'girlfriend' or just 'female friend' in Slovak.

scenarios.situation: What is the collective Slovak word for 'brothers and sisters' (siblings)?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Súrodenci = siblings (brothers and sisters collectively). Rodičia = parents, Manželia = married couple / spouses, Deti = children. 'Máš súrodencov?' (Do you have siblings?) is a very common getting-to-know-you question in Slovakia.

scenarios.situation: How do you say 'We are engaged' in Slovak?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Sme zasnúbení = We are engaged. Zasnúbiť sa = to get engaged. Slovak engagements often involve the male asking the female's father for permission — a tradition still common in many families. Zásnuby = engagement party.

scenarios.situation: In Slovak, a married man says 'Som ženatý.' What would a married woman say?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Vydatá = married (for women). Ženatý = married (for men). These are completely different words! This reflects Slovak's grammatical gender system. A common mistake for learners is saying 'Som ženatá' — that form doesn't exist. A woman is vydatá, a man is ženatý.

items.sentence: Toto je môj ___. Volá sa Peter a má dvadsaťpäť rokov.
items.translation: This is my ___. His name is Peter and he is twenty-five years old.

items.sentence: Mám troch ___: dvoch bratov a jednu sestru.
items.translation: I have three ___: two brothers and one sister.

items.sentence: Moji ___ bývajú v Bratislave — mama a otec.
items.translation: My ___ live in Bratislava — mum and dad.
I'm ___. I don't have a boyfriend or husband.

items.sentence: Naša ___ má päť členov: mama, otec, brat, sestra a ja.
items.translation: Our ___ has five members: mum, dad, brother, sister and me.

items.sentence: Môj ___ žije v Londýne. Je to brat mojej manželky.
items.translation: My ___ lives in London. He is my wife's brother.

items.sentence: Sme ___. Svadba bude na jar budúci rok.
items.translation: We are ___. The wedding will be next spring.

items.sentence: Mám ___ — chodíme spolu pol roka. Ešte nie sme zasnúbení.
items.translation: I have a ___ — we've been together for half a year. Not engaged yet.

items.sentence: Toto je môj ___. Volá sa Peter a má dvadsaťpäť rokov.
Mám troch ___: dvoch bratov a jednu sestru.
Moji ___ bývajú v Bratislave — mama a otec.
Naša ___ má päť členov: mama, otec, brat, sestra a ja.
Môj ___ žije v Londýne. Je to brat mojej manželky.
Sme ___. Svadba bude na jar budúci rok.
Mám ___ — chodíme spolu pol roka. Ešte nie sme zasnúbení.

exercises.scenario: ☕ You are having coffee with your Slovak neighbour Jana. She asks about your family.

turns.snailMessage: Odkiaľ si? Máš tu nejakú rodinu?
turns.snailMessageEnglish: Where are you from? Do you have any family here?

choices.slovak: Nie, moja rodina je doma. Mám tu iba manželku.
choices.english: No, my family is back home. I only have my wife here.
I don't want to talk about family.
Where are you from?

turns.snailReply: To je romantické! Kde ste sa s manželkou spoznali?
turns.snailReplyEnglish: How romantic! Where did you and your wife meet?

choices.slovak: Spoznali sme sa v Londýne. Ona je Slovenka a ja som z Anglicka.
choices.english: We met in London. She is Slovak and I'm from England.
I don't understand the question.
That's private information.

turns.snailReply: Ach, takže si ženatý so Slovenkou! Máte deti?
turns.snailReplyEnglish: Oh, so you're married to a Slovak woman! Do you have children?

choices.slovak: Zatiaľ nie, ale plánujeme rodinu o pár rokov.
choices.english: Not yet, but we plan to start a family in a few years.

choices.slovak: Deti sú pre nás príliš drahé.
choices.english: Children are too expensive for us.
That's a personal question.

turns.snailReply: Super! A máš súrodencov doma v Anglicku?
turns.snailReplyEnglish: Great! And do you have siblings back home in England?

choices.slovak: Áno, mám staršieho brata a mladšiu sestru.
choices.english: Yes, I have an older brother and a younger sister.
I'm not interested in this question.
I only have friends, not siblings.

turns.snailReply: A kde bývajú? Tiež v Anglicku?
turns.snailReplyEnglish: And where do they live? Also in England?

choices.slovak: Brat býva v Manchesteri a sestra sa presťahovala do Austrálie.
choices.english: My brother lives in Manchester and my sister moved to Australia.
I don't know where they live.
They all live with my parents.

turns.snailReply: Austrália! To je ďaleko. A tvoji rodičia?
turns.snailReplyEnglish: Australia! That's far away. And your parents?

choices.slovak: Moji rodičia bývajú v Oxforde. Navštevujem ich raz za rok.
choices.english: My parents live in Oxford. I visit them once a year.
My parents are very old.

choices.slovak: Rodičia nechcú, aby som ich navštevoval.
choices.english: My parents don't want me to visit them.

turns.snailReply: Rozumiem. Moja babička hovorí, že rodina je vždy najdôležitejšia.
turns.snailReplyEnglish: I understand. My grandma says family is always the most important thing.

choices.slovak: Súhlasím. Chýba mi moja rodina, ale Slovensko je teraz môj domov.
choices.english: I agree. I miss my family, but Slovakia is my home now.
Grandmas always say such things.

choices.slovak: Nie, práca je dôležitejšia ako rodina.
choices.english: No, work is more important than family.

turns.snailReply: To je krásne! Tvoja manželka má šťastie. A má ona súrodencov tu?
turns.snailReplyEnglish: That's beautiful! Your wife is lucky. And does she have siblings here?

choices.slovak: Áno, má sestru a brata. Jej rodina ma prijala veľmi dobre.
choices.english: Yes, she has a sister and a brother. Her family accepted me very well.
Her siblings don't like me.
I don't know her family.

turns.snailReply: To je nádherné! Vitaj v Slovensku a v tejto veľkej rodine!
turns.snailReplyEnglish: That's wonderful! Welcome to Slovakia and to this big family!

turns.snailMessage: Odkiaľ si? Máš tu nejakú rodinu?

choices.slovak: Spoznali sme sa v Londýne. Ona je Slovenka a ja som z Anglicka.

turns.snailReply: Ach, takže si ženatý so Slovenkou! Máte deti?

choices.slovak: Zatiaľ nie, ale plánujeme rodinu o pár rokov.
Deti sú pre nás príliš drahé.

turns.snailReply: A kde bývajú? Tiež v Anglicku?

choices.slovak: Brat býva v Manchesteri a sestra sa presťahovala do Austrálie.

choices.slovak: Moji rodičia bývajú v Oxforde. Navštevujem ich raz za rok.
Rodičia nechcú, aby som ich navštevoval.
